role_summary: SNCC field secretary; chairman of the Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party (MFDP); beaten in the June 1963 Winona, Mississippi jail alongside Fannie Lou Hamer; later DC community-organizing leader.
ai_reading: Guyot's top semantic neighbor is Charles McLaurin (entry 17, count 162), the SNCC Sunflower County project director; the next four are John and Jean Rosenberg (entry 61), Ekwueme Michael Thelwell (entry 34), the Ladner sisters joint interview (entry 75), and Courtland Cox (entry 24). The cluster centers on the Mississippi voter-registration network and the SNCC Washington-and-MFDP coalition that Guyot's MFDP chairmanship organized. His Southern Struggle vs Northern Struggle position at +0.44 confirms the Mississippi anchor. The novel observation is the absence of Euvester Simpson (entry 41) from his top five: Simpson's own page documents that she joined SNCC in 1962 after Guyot vetted her with her father, and the two were beaten together in the Winona jail in June 1963; their oral histories are obviously connected biographically, but the embedding does not pull them into nearest-neighbor proximity, suggesting their interview transcripts emphasize different periods or vocabularies of the same movement work.
biographical_paragraph: Guyot served as a SNCC field secretary in Mississippi in the early 1960s and rose to chair the Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party, the parallel party that mounted the 1964 Atlantic City convention challenge against the regular all-white Mississippi Democratic delegation [src: 2]. In June 1963 he was arrested with Fannie Lou Hamer, June Johnson, Euvester Simpson, and Annell Ponder at the Winona, Mississippi bus station; the beatings he suffered in the jail were among the documented injuries of the Mississippi Summer Project [src: 2]. After the movement years he relocated to Washington, D.C., where he was active in community organizing through the 1980s and 1990s; his papers are held at the DC Public Library Washingtoniana Collection [src: 3]. He was born July 17, 1939 in Pass Christian, Mississippi, and died November 23, 2012 at seventy-three; the Washington Post obituary by Hamil R. Harris and Matt Schudel records the dates [src: 2]. His Library of Congress oral history is entry 76 of the Civil Rights History Project (American Folklife Center accession AFC 2010/039); the interview was conducted by Julian Bond on December 30, 2010 [src: 1].

sources.title: Lawrence Guyot oral history (interview by Julian Bond, December 30, 2010)
